lessons.title: States of Matter and Changes of State

content.body: Matter exists in three common states: solid, liquid, and gas. Changes between states involve energy transfer and can be explained by the kinetic molecular theory.

Changes of state:
Solid → Liquid: melting (absorbs energy)
Liquid → Gas: evaporation/boiling (absorbs energy)
Gas → Liquid: condensation (releases energy)
Liquid → Solid: freezing/solidification (releases energy)
Solid → Gas: sublimation (e.g. dry ice CO₂, iodine)
Gas → Solid: deposition

content.title: Heating Curves
content.body:
During heating:
1. Temperature rises (solid warms)
2. Flat section: melting (energy breaks bonds, T constant)
3. Temperature rises (liquid warms)
4. Flat section: boiling (energy breaks liquid, T constant)
5. Temperature rises (gas warms)

The flat sections are the latent heat regions

Latent heat:
Specific latent heat of fusion (L_f): energy to melt 1kg
Specific latent heat of vaporisation (L_v): energy to vaporise 1kg
Q = mL
Water: L_f = 334 kJ/kg, L_v = 2260 kJ/kg

questions.question: During melting, temperature
options: remains constant while energy is absorbed to break bonds
questions.answer: remains constant while energy is absorbed to break bonds
questions.explanation: At the melting point, energy input breaks intermolecular bonds rather than raising temperature — hence the flat section.

questions.question: Sublimation is
questions.explanation: Sublimation: solid converts directly to gas without passing through liquid phase (dry ice, iodine, moth balls).

questions.question: The specific latent heat of vaporisation of water is greater than fusion because
options: much more energy is needed to completely separate molecules in gas than just loosen them in liquid
questions.answer: much more energy is needed to completely separate molecules in gas than just loosen them in liquid
questions.explanation: Vaporisation separates molecules completely (overcome all intermolecular attraction). Fusion only loosens the solid structure.

questions.question: Q = mL calculates
options: heat absorbed or released during change of state
questions.answer: heat absorbed or released during change of state
questions.explanation: Q = mL: Q = energy (J), m = mass (kg), L = specific latent heat (J/kg)

questions.question: Energy needed to vaporise 2kg of water (L_v = 2260 kJ/kg)
Condensation releases energy because
options: gas particles form bonds with other particles releasing potential energy as they become liquid
questions.answer: gas particles form bonds with other particles releasing potential energy as they become liquid
questions.explanation: As gas particles come together and form intermolecular bonds, they release the energy that was used to separate them.

questions.question: Evaporation occurs at
questions.explanation: Evaporation happens at any temperature as high-energy surface molecules escape. Boiling is evaporation throughout the liquid at boiling point.

questions.question: Sweating cools the body because
options: evaporation of sweat from skin takes latent heat from the body
questions.answer: evaporation of sweat from skin takes latent heat from the body
questions.explanation: Evaporating sweat absorbs latent heat of vaporisation from skin surface, cooling the body.

questions.question: Why does a solid have a definite shape?
options: Particles are held in fixed positions by strong intermolecular forces
There is no space between particles
questions.answer: Particles are held in fixed positions by strong intermolecular forces
questions.explanation: In solids, intermolecular forces are strong enough to keep particles in fixed positions — giving definite shape and volume.

questions.question: During boiling, all energy input goes to
options: breaking intermolecular forces to convert liquid to gas
questions.answer: breaking intermolecular forces to convert liquid to gas
questions.explanation: At boiling point, energy (latent heat) overcomes intermolecular attractive forces, enabling liquid molecules to escape as gas.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Density:
ρ = m/V
ρ = density (kg/m³ or g/cm³)
m = mass (kg or g), V = volume (m³ or cm³)

Water: ρ = 1000 kg/m³ = 1 g/cm³
Objects less dense than water float
Objects denser than water sink

content.title: Archimedes Principle
content.body:
An object immersed in fluid experiences an upthrust (buoyant force) equal to the weight of fluid displaced.

Upthrust = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g

Floating: upthrust = weight of object
Sinking: upthrust < weight

Why ships float despite steel being denser:
Hollow shape displaces large volume of water → large upthrust

content.title: Density Calculations
content.body:
Density of object with mass 500g and volume 200cm³:
ρ = 500/200 = 2.5 g/cm³ (denser than water, sinks)

Volume of 3kg of aluminium (ρ = 2700 kg/m³):
V = m/ρ = 3/2700 = 0.00111 m³ = 1111 cm³

questions.question: Density formula is
questions.explanation: Density = mass / volume. SI unit: kg/m³

questions.question: Density of 200g object with volume 50cm³
Object with ρ=0.8 g/cm³ in water (ρ=1 g/cm³)
questions.explanation: Object less dense than fluid → floats. 0.8 < 1 g/cm³.

questions.question: Archimedes principle: upthrust equals
questions.explanation: Archimedes: buoyant force = weight of fluid displaced by the submerged object.

questions.question: A ship made of steel floats because
options: steel is less dense than water
the hollow shape displaces water equal in weight to the whole ship
questions.answer: the hollow shape displaces water equal in weight to the whole ship
questions.explanation: A ship's hollow hull displaces a large volume of water. The water's weight (upthrust) equals the ship's total weight.

questions.question: Mass of 500cm³ of iron (ρ=7.9 g/cm³)
To find volume of irregular solid experimentally
questions.explanation: An irregular solid's volume = volume of water displaced when fully submerged in a measuring cylinder.

questions.question: Upthrust on object in water depends on
options: volume of fluid displaced and fluid density
questions.answer: volume of fluid displaced and fluid density
questions.explanation: Upthrust = ρ_fluid × V_displaced × g. Depends on how much fluid is displaced and the fluid's density.

questions.question: Why does a hot air balloon float?
options: Heated air expands, becomes less dense than surrounding cool air, so balloon+air is lighter
questions.answer: Heated air expands, becomes less dense than surrounding cool air, so balloon+air is lighter
questions.explanation: Hot air is less dense than cool air. If balloon + hot air < weight of displaced cool air, balloon rises.

questions.question: An ice cube floats in water. The fraction submerged ≈
questions.explanation: Ice density ≈ 0.92 g/cm³. Fraction submerged = ρ_ice/ρ_water = 0.92. About 90% is underwater.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

content.body:
Hooke's Law: F = ke
F = applied force (N)
k = spring constant (N/m)
e = extension (m)

Valid only within elastic limit
Beyond elastic limit = permanent deformation

content.title: Spring Constant
content.body:
Spring stretched 5cm by 20N force.
k = F/e = 20/0.05 = 400 N/m
Extension for 30N: e = 30/400 = 0.075m = 7.5cm
Elastic materials return to original shape when force removed. Plastic materials permanently deform. The elastic limit is the maximum force that allows elastic behavior.

questions.question: Hooke's Law states F =
questions.explanation: F = ke where k is spring constant and e is extension.

questions.question: A spring extends 4cm when 8N is applied. Spring constant =
Elastic limit is
options: Point beyond which material permanently deforms
questions.answer: Point beyond which material permanently deforms
questions.explanation: Beyond the elastic limit, Hooke's Law no longer applies and the material is permanently deformed.

questions.question: Spring constant k has units
questions.explanation: k = F/e so units are N/m.

questions.question: Two identical springs in series with force F. Extension compared to one spring is
questions.explanation: Springs in series share the force, each extends the same, total extension doubles.

questions.question: Two springs in parallel. Effective spring constant
questions.explanation: Parallel springs share the load, making system stiffer (k doubles).

questions.question: A rubber band shows elastic behaviour if
questions.explanation: Elastic means it returns to original shape after force is removed.

questions.question: Graph of F vs extension for Hooke's Law is
questions.explanation: F=ke gives a straight line through origin with gradient k.

questions.question: Beyond elastic limit on F-e graph, the line
options: Curves and doesn't return to origin
questions.answer: Curves and doesn't return to origin
questions.explanation: Beyond elastic limit, deformation is permanent - plastic region.

questions.question: Which material is most elastic
questions.explanation: Steel returns very precisely to original shape within its elastic limit - used in springs.

content.body: Surface tension is a force acting along the surface of a liquid, caused by cohesive forces between liquid molecules. It makes water form droplets and allows insects to walk on water.

Viscosity: resistance to flow
Higher viscosity = thicker, slower flow
Unit: Pascal-second (Pa·s)

Surface tension γ = F/L
F = force, L = length of surface

content.title: Surface Tension Effect
content.body: A needle placed carefully on water floats even though steel is 7× denser than water. Surface tension acts as an elastic film supporting the needle's weight.

questions.question: Surface tension is caused by
options: Cohesive forces between molecules at surface
questions.answer: Cohesive forces between molecules at surface
questions.explanation: Surface molecules have unbalanced cohesive forces pulling inward, creating surface tension.

questions.question: Which demonstrates surface tension
questions.explanation: A steel needle floats on water due to surface tension supporting its weight.

questions.question: Viscosity is a measure of
questions.explanation: Viscosity describes how thick or resistant to flow a fluid is.

questions.question: Honey has higher viscosity than water, meaning
questions.explanation: Higher viscosity = greater resistance to flow = slower movement.

questions.question: Adding detergent to water
questions.explanation: Detergent molecules disrupt cohesive forces between water molecules, reducing surface tension.

questions.question: Water forms spherical droplets because
questions.explanation: Surface tension pulls liquid into minimum surface area shape - a sphere.

questions.question: Capillary action (water rising in thin tube) is caused by
options: Adhesion to tube walls and surface tension
questions.answer: Adhesion to tube walls and surface tension
questions.explanation: Water adheres to glass tube walls and surface tension pulls column up.

questions.question: At higher temperature, viscosity of liquids
questions.explanation: Higher temperature gives molecules more energy to overcome cohesive forces, reducing viscosity.

questions.question: Which has highest viscosity
questions.explanation: Glycerol is very viscous - used as a lubricant and in medicines.

questions.question: Mercury in glass tube curves
questions.explanation: Mercury has stronger cohesion than adhesion to glass, so it curves downward - opposite to water.

content.body: In this lesson, we explore advanced aspects of Properties Matter. Build on what you have already learned and apply your knowledge to more challenging problems.
Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Properties Matter concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Properties Matter builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 5.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Properties Matter. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 5.2
content.body: Another type of problem you may encounter: Questions may test your ability to combine multiple concepts within Properties Matter. Practice identifying the type of problem and the appropriate method before attempting to solve.

Common examination questions on Properties Matter include: definitions and explanations, numerical calculations, diagram interpretation, and analysis of given data. Make sure you can handle all types.
Summary and Review

You have now covered all lessons in Properties Matter. Review the key concepts from each lesson, practice past paper questions, and ensure you can apply your knowledge confidently. Good luck in your studies!

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
